tweets.title:
Pakistan Defence Minister Khawaja Muhammad Asif has indicated the possibility of expanding the regional defence and economic arrangement with Qatar and Turkiye, suggesting that the framework could evolve
into a broader security pact aimed at strengthening regional cooperation.

Pakistan, last year, had already signed a "Strategic Mutual Defence Agreement" with Saudi Arabia, pledging that any aggression against either nation would be treated as an attack on both. 

India has also issued a response in the wake of the "Strategic Mutual Defence Agreement" between Saudi Arabia and Pakistan, stating it will closely study the pact's implications for India's national security and regional and global stability.

tweets.title:
Some say Modi Government is still not releasing funds for the Super Sukhoi program, around ₹70,000 crores, and only news outlets keep reporting that it will happen.  
Others say, “Since 2018 I’ve been listening to this Super Sukhoi nonsense.”

✅ First we need to understand that the new Super Sukhoi upgrade is not your grandfather-era Super Sukhoi. 
✅ This upgrade will take the MKI to a completely different level with a GaN-based AESA radar, something even current Rafale variants do not have. 
✅ Add to that a new EW suite, digital cockpit, MAWS, a host of new weapons, and probably even a new engine in the future.

If you monitor global fighter programs, you’ll realize the world was still talking about Rafale F4 when many here had already started discussing Super Sukhoi. However, the extent and scope of this upgrade is closer to Rafale F5-level ambition than a routine mid-life upgrade.

Technology of this sort takes time. Virupaksha has only recently gone onto the test jig. Many of these blocks are still under development and validation. They are not yet at the stage where GOI will release full-fledged fund flow for mass execution.

As milestones are achieved, the funds will flow.

There is also a reason why restarting and expanding the Su-30 line has taken time. This is not a simple upgrade packag, it is practically the evolution of the MKI into an entirely new generation of capability.

Lashkar terrorist killed as Army stymied infiltration bid along LoC in J&K

The Indian Army on Tuesday foiled a major infiltration attempt by suspected Pakistani terrorists along the Line of Control (LoC) in Jammu and Kashmir, killing one infiltrator in the operation.

A heavy exchange of fire is underway between the infiltrators and Army troops deployed in the Krishna Ghati sector of Poonch's Mendhar.

Persistent surveillance detected suspicious movement,  of a group of heavily armed terrorists attempting to cross the LoC under the cover of darkness. The infiltrators were challenged, following which they opened fire, prompting retaliation from the Army and triggering an intense gunfight. 

“The troops continue to dominate the area and maintain a high state of operational readiness across the sector,” the Indian Army said.

RT by @OsintTV: बीकानेर बॉर्डर पर क्रैश हुए ड्रोन के वैज्ञानिक और डिजिटल विश्लेषण ने उगले पाकिस्तान प्रायोजित हथियार एवं नार्को तस्करी नेटवर्क के सनसनीखेज राज; बीएसएफ, बीकानेर पुलिस और पंजाब पुलिस की संयुक्त जांच में खुलासा—पाकिस्तान से उड़ान भरने वाला ड्रोन पहले भी बीकानेर सहित राजस्थान और पंजाब के सीमावर्ती इलाकों में कई बार हथियार और नशे की खेप गिरा चुका था, फरीदकोट जेल से गिरफ्तार हुआ ड्रोन ड्रॉपिंग सिंडिकेट का कथित मास्टरमाइंड सुखविंदर उर्फ सोनू
A major breakthrough has emerged from Rajasthan’s Bikaner border, where the scientific and digital analysis of a crashed drone has exposed a Pakistan-backed transnational arms and narcotics smuggling network. In a coordinated operation involving the Border Security Force (BSF), Bikaner Police, and Punjab Police, investigators traced the February 26, 2026 drone drop near Himagiri Border Outpost in Khajuwala to an organized syndicate that allegedly used UAVs to deliver pistols, ammunition, and narcotics into Indian territory.

The recovered drone was found carrying five pistols and nearly 300 live cartridges. What turned this case into a landmark investigation was the forensic exploitation of the drone’s onboard data. BSF intelligence specialists extracted GPS logs, route history, and previous mission data, revealing that the same drone had reportedly conducted multiple drops in Bikaner, other parts of Rajasthan, and Punjab.

The investigation led to the arrest of Sukhwinder alias Sonu, described by officials as a key kingpin of the drone-dropping network. Already lodged in Faridkot Jail in NDPS and heroin trafficking cases, Sonu was brought on production warrant by Bikaner Police. A mobile phone recovered from his possession is now being analyzed for links to Pakistan-based handlers and other operatives across India.

Officials say the case highlights the growing threat of drone-enabled narco-terror along India’s western frontier and demonstrates how inter-agency cooperation and scientific analysis are helping dismantle sophisticated cross-border smuggling syndicates.

tweets.title:
Peneddown some thoughts; 
A week ago during a Space, I said the “mine of the world”, Africa is changing fast. The statement may sound dramatic, but the numbers increasingly support it.

Africa today holds nearly 30% of the world’s known mineral reserves, including massive deposits of cobalt, lithium, manganese, uranium, rare earths, platinum, and copper, all essential for EVs, semiconductors, batteries, aerospace systems, and modern defence industries. The Democratic Republic of Congo alone contributes around 70% of global cobalt production, while countries like Zambia, Namibia, and Zimbabwe are becoming central to future energy and technology supply chains.

Historically, every industrial transition has been accompanied by a resource race. Britain dominated the coal era, the United States rose during the oil-industrial age after World War II, and now the (AI, battery, semiconductor) era is triggering a new scramble for strategic minerals. China recognized this almost two decades ago. Today it controls or heavily influences large portions of global rare earth processing and significant mining infrastructure across Africa through long-term investments, logistics corridors, and debt-backed development models.

What is interesting is that India too has quietly started increasing its footprint across Africa through mining agreements, fertilizer investments, energy partnerships, and diplomatic outreach. Not remotely at Chinese scale yet, but enough to indicate that New Delhi understands the strategic direction of the world economy.

The concern, however, is that this global transition is happening during a period of geopolitical instability. Historically, prolonged conflicts have almost always reshaped economic systems. The 1973 oil crisis caused stagflation across Western economies. The Gulf War disrupted energy markets. COVID exposed how fragile “just-in-time” global supply chains really were. Now ongoing wars across multiple regions threaten energy flows, shipping lanes, insurance costs, and commodity pricing simultaneously.

If current conflicts continue for months, oil volatility alone can trigger cascading effects across transportation, manufacturing, fertilizers, aviation, food inflation, and consumer markets. Nearly every major economy remains interconnected through maritime trade routes, making disruption contagious.

Equally important is the structural shift in global power. A declining superpower has historically created periods of instability before a new balance emerges. The decline of the British Empire after World War II reshaped global finance and colonial systems. Today many analysts argue the US-led unipolar order is weakening under debt pressures, domestic polarization, trade wars, and strategic overextension. Policies and actions under Donald Trump accelerated several protectionist and de-globalization trends, but no single nation appears capable of replacing American dominance outright.

Instead, the world is entering a multipolar phase, where power is distributed between the United States, China, India, Russia, Europe, the Gulf economies, ASEAN, and emerging regional blocs. Historically, multipolar systems tend to be economically competitive but geopolitically unstable.

At an individual level, ordinary people are already feeling the consequences. Red portfolios, inflationary pressure, rising living costs, stagnant wage growth in many sectors, and declining purchasing power parity are becoming visible across both developed and developing economies. If these structural issues continue through the decade, consumer behaviour, investment patterns, migration trends, and even political systems could undergo major transformation.
tweets.content:
Peneddown some thoughts;
A week ago during a Space, I said the “mine of the world”, Africa is changing fast. The statement may sound dramatic, but the numbers increasingly support it.

Africa today holds nearly 30% of the world’s known mineral reserves, including massive deposits of cobalt, lithium, manganese, uranium, rare earths, platinum, and copper, all essential for EVs, semiconductors, batteries, aerospace systems, and modern defence industries. The Democratic Republic of Congo alone contributes around 70% of global cobalt production, while countries like Zambia, Namibia, and Zimbabwe are becoming central to future energy and technology supply chains.

Historically, every industrial transition has been accompanied by a resource race. Britain dominated the coal era, the United States rose during the oil-industrial age after World War II, and now the (AI, battery, semiconductor) era is triggering a new scramble for strategic minerals. China recognized this almost two decades ago. Today it controls or heavily influences large portions of global rare earth processing and significant mining infrastructure across Africa through long-term investments, logistics corridors, and debt-backed development models.

What is interesting is that India too has quietly started increasing its footprint across Africa through mining agreements, fertilizer investments, energy partnerships, and diplomatic outreach. Not remotely at Chinese scale yet, but enough to indicate that New Delhi understands the strategic direction of the world economy.

The concern, however, is that this global transition is happening during a period of geopolitical instability. Historically, prolonged conflicts have almost always reshaped economic systems. The 1973 oil crisis caused stagflation across Western economies. The Gulf War disrupted energy markets. COVID exposed how fragile “just-in-time” global supply chains really were. Now ongoing wars across multiple regions threaten energy flows, shipping lanes, insurance costs, and commodity pricing simultaneously.

If current conflicts continue for months, oil volatility alone can trigger cascading effects across transportation, manufacturing, fertilizers, aviation, food inflation, and consumer markets. Nearly every major economy remains interconnected through maritime trade routes, making disruption contagious.

Equally important is the structural shift in global power. A declining superpower has historically created periods of instability before a new balance emerges. The decline of the British Empire after World War II reshaped global finance and colonial systems. Today many analysts argue the US-led unipolar order is weakening under debt pressures, domestic polarization, trade wars, and strategic overextension. Policies and actions under Donald Trump accelerated several protectionist and de-globalization trends, but no single nation appears capable of replacing American dominance outright.

Instead, the world is entering a multipolar phase, where power is distributed between the United States, China, India, Russia, Europe, the Gulf economies, ASEAN, and emerging regional blocs. Historically, multipolar systems tend to be economically competitive but geopolitically unstable.

At an individual level, ordinary people are already feeling the consequences. Red portfolios, inflationary pressure, rising living costs, stagnant wage growth in many sectors, and declining purchasing power parity are becoming visible across both developed and developing economies. If these structural issues continue through the decade, consumer behaviour, investment patterns, migration trends, and even political systems could undergo major transformation.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: The first test was planned on 11 May. The thermonuclear device was placed in a shaft code named White House, which was approximately 230m deep, the fission bomb was placed in a 150m deep shaft code named Taj Mahal, and the first sub-kiloton device in shaft Kumbhkaran.
The timing of the tests was pursuant to the local weather conditions, and the test sequence was initiated in the afternoon.

National Technology Day | Pokhran-II Nuclear Tests

National Technology Day is celebrated in India annually on May 11 to commemorate the successful Pokhran-II nuclear tests ("Operation Shakti") of 1998, marking a major milestone in indigenous technology.
It honors scientists and engineers, with the 2026 theme focusing on "Responsible Innovation for Inclusive Growth".
